title: EAGLE: Large-scale Learning of Turbulent Fluid Dynamics with Mesh Transformers

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Results show that the new method outperforms the previous works.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Although different shapes of the boundary affect how the mesh is constructed and how they are clustered, it seems that the model still generalizes well.
2. The results are promising and supportive of the technical contribution.

Weakness:
1. Missing reference: Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 7. 2021.
2. I wonder how this can be applied to 3D cases. Since the 2D task only there is already 270Gb data, it is probably not practical to prepare a good amount of data for the model to converge and generalize. Also, dealing with tetrahedra instead of triangles may introduce unknown challenges. Please add more insights regarding the extension to 3D in the conclusion section.
3. How does the mesh resolution affect the training/test accuracy? If the training and test images are of different resolutions, will the model perform better or worse? It would be great to see such comparisons in the experiments.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: See above.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I would recommend weak reject because of the weaknesses described above. I am happy to change my score if the issues are addressed in the rebuttal.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Results show that the new method outperforms the previous works.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Although different shapes of the boundary affect how the mesh is constructed and how they are clustered, it seems that the model still generalizes well.
2. The results are promising and supportive of the technical contribution.

Weakness:
1. Missing reference: Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 7. 2021.
2. I wonder how this can be applied to 3D cases. Since the 2D task only there is already 270Gb data, it is probably not practical to prepare a good amount of data for the model to converge and generalize. Also, dealing with tetrahedra instead of triangles may introduce unknown challenges. Please add more insights regarding the extension to 3D in the conclusion section.
3. How does the mesh resolution affect the training/test accuracy? If the training and test images are of different resolutions, will the model perform better or worse? It would be great to see such comparisons in the experiments.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above.

Summary Of The Review:
I would recommend weak reject because of the weaknesses described above. I am happy to change my score if the issues are addressed in the rebuttal.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Submission:**
This paper introduces a novel dataset specifically designed for predicting fluid turbulence. Accompanying this dataset, the authors propose a Transformer-based method to model the complex dynamics of fluids. A key innovation in their architecture involves the integration of clustering and graph pooling mechanisms positioned before the attention block, which serves to expand the receptive field beyond a strictly local neighborhood. The experimental results presented demonstrate that this new approach achieves superior performance compared to previously established methods.

**Assessment of Strengths:**
1.  A significant strength of the presented model is its commendable generalization capability. This is particularly noteworthy given that variations in boundary shapes inherently influence both mesh construction and the subsequent clustering process.
2.  The empirical results reported are highly promising and effectively substantiate the technical contributions put forth by the authors.

**Identification of Weaknesses:**
1.  A critical omission in the related work section is the reference to "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control" by Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. (Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2021), which appears to be highly relevant to the scope of this research.
2.  The paper offers insufficient discussion regarding the practical applicability and scalability of the proposed method to three-dimensional scenarios. Considering that the 2D task alone requires a substantial 270GB of data, the feasibility of generating an adequate amount of data for 3D applications to ensure proper model convergence and generalization appears questionable. Furthermore, the transition from managing triangular meshes to tetrahedral structures in 3D could introduce unforeseen complexities. It would be beneficial for the authors to include more comprehensive insights into the potential extension of this work to 3D cases, perhaps within the conclusion section.
3.  The experimental evaluation would be significantly strengthened by an analysis of how different mesh resolutions impact both training and test accuracy. Specifically, it is important to investigate the model's performance when training and testing are conducted on data with disparate resolutions, providing a clearer understanding of its robustness in varied conditions. Such comparisons should be incorporated into the experiments.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**
The assessment of these aspects is primarily reflected in the detailed strengths and weaknesses outlined above.

**Overall Assessment:**
While the paper undeniably presents interesting contributions and promising results, the identified weaknesses are substantial enough to lead to a recommendation of "weak reject" at this stage. I believe that addressing these concerns—specifically the missing reference, the practical scalability to 3D, and a thorough analysis of mesh resolution's impact—is crucial for enhancing the work's completeness and broader scientific impact. My numerical rating, which is marginally above the acceptance threshold (6), reflects the inherent potential of this research and my willingness to reconsider this recommendation should these critical issues be adequately addressed and elaborated upon in a rebuttal.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

## Rewritten Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This submission introduces a novel Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture alongside a new benchmark dataset designed for analyzing 2D turbulent fluid dynamics generated by a solitary moving flow source. The proposed methodology, which incorporates autoregressive modeling and self-attention mechanisms, demonstrates superior performance on the evaluation dataset.

**Strength And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
*   The manuscript is clearly articulated, with the algorithms presented in an understandable and accessible manner.
*   The experimental outcomes are impressive, showcasing strong performance both qualitatively and quantitatively.
*   The experimental setup is thorough, effectively illustrating the robust nature of the proposed approach.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A key limitation lies in the reliance on a simulated dataset, generated by an existing fluid dynamics simulator. This implies that the accuracy of the model's predictions is inherently tied to the fidelity and quality of the underlying simulator.
*   Given that the current work is exclusively based on 2D simulations and lacks real-world experimental validation, it remains uncertain how effectively this model would generalize or perform in more complex 3D real-scene environments. A brief discussion on potential real-world applications or, ideally, the inclusion of some preliminary real-world results would significantly enhance the paper's impact.
*   The paper would benefit from a dedicated section discussing potential failure modes or providing an analysis of challenging cases where the model might not perform optimally.

**Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:**
Overall, I find the quality of this paper to be commendable.

**Summary Of The Review:**
To summarize, this paper is commendably written and presents compelling results for 2D dynamic fluid analysis. I recommend that the authors consider adding a concise paragraph exploring potential real-world applications and including a discussion of failure cases to further strengthen the submission.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This manuscript proposed a method to train neural networks to predict turbulent fluid dynamics. For the neural architecture, this manuscript introduced the concept of a transformer into the fluid dynamics field, which outperforms previous methods due to long-range receptive fields. To support the training of the mesh transformer, this manuscript generates a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset using a controlled drone. The experiments showed the superior performance of their method over several different baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- The dataset is large-scale. It covers a big fraction of the interest of the physical datasets. Previous datasets are mostly steady-state simulations.
- The manuscript compared their method with several well-known baselines.

Weaknesses:
- In my opinion, the weaknesses are obvious. First, the dataset only contains 2D data, whose usefulness is suspicious due to the diffusion of fluid. I wonder if the authors can provide more insights on how they are useful in real engineering.
- Several previous datasets are working in CFD, e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/. How is the proposed dataset compared to theirs?
- The only variation in the dataset is the geometry. However, in reality, there are more descriptions of fluid (it is true even for air itself), e.g., the Reynolds number, the temperature, the boundary conditions, and the wind speed. I wonder how generalizable a network trained on this dataset can be.

Summary Of The Paper:
This manuscript proposed a method to train neural networks to predict turbulent fluid dynamics. For the neural architecture, this manuscript introduced the concept of a transformer into the fluid dynamics field, which outperforms previous methods due to long-range receptive fields. To support the training of the mesh transformer, this manuscript generates a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset using a controlled drone. The experiments showed the superior performance of their method over several different baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
- The dataset is large-scale. It covers a big fraction of the interest of the physical datasets. Previous datasets are mostly steady-state simulations.
- The manuscript compared their method with several well-known baselines.

Weaknesses:
- In my opinion, the weaknesses are obvious. First, the dataset only contains 2D data, whose usefulness is suspicious due to the diffusion of fluid. I wonder if the authors can provide more insights on how they are useful in real engineering.
- Several previous datasets are working in CFD, e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/. How is the proposed dataset compared to theirs?
- The only variation in the dataset is the geometry. However, in reality, there are more descriptions of fluid (it is true even for air itself), e.g., the Reynolds number, the temperature, the boundary conditions, and the wind speed. I wonder how generalizable a network trained on this dataset can be.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The manuscript is well-written. I followed their motivation and method.

Summary Of The Review:
The dataset is valuable to the community. However, I would like to see the authors justify my questions about the dataset. I am open to changing the score based on the response.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

## Rewritten Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This manuscript presents an innovative methodology for training neural networks to forecast turbulent fluid dynamics. A key contribution is the integration of a transformer architecture into the field of fluid dynamics, which the authors demonstrate to surpass prior methods through its ability to capture long-range dependencies. To support the development of this mesh transformer, the study involved the creation of a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset, generated using a controlled drone. Experimental results consistently illustrate the proposed method's superior performance when compared against a range of established baselines.

**Strength And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
*   The newly introduced dataset is noteworthy for its considerable scale and its ability to encompass a significant range of physical phenomena, particularly contrasting with many prior datasets that primarily focused on steady-state simulations.
*   The experimental validation is robust, featuring comprehensive comparisons against a selection of widely recognized baseline methods.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A primary concern lies with the dataset's exclusive reliance on 2D data. Given the inherent three-dimensional nature and diffusion characteristics of real-world fluid dynamics, the practical utility and applicability of a model trained solely on 2D information for real engineering scenarios remain questionable. Further elucidation from the authors on the real-world relevance of this 2D data would be highly beneficial.
*   The review lacks a comparative analysis against established computational fluid dynamics (CFD) datasets, such as the widely used turbulence database from Johns Hopkins University (e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/). A discussion on how the proposed dataset aligns or differs from these existing resources would strengthen the work.
*   The dataset currently exhibits variation only in geometric configurations. However, realistic fluid dynamics involve numerous other critical parameters, including but not limited to Reynolds number, temperature, diverse boundary conditions, and varying wind speeds. This limited scope of variation raises significant questions regarding the generalizability of a neural network trained exclusively on this dataset to more complex, real-world fluid environments.

**Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:**
The manuscript is well-structured and clearly articulated, allowing for an easy understanding of the authors' motivation and methodological approach.

**Summary Of The Review:**
Overall, the contribution of this dataset to the research community holds significant promise. Nevertheless, it is imperative that the authors thoroughly address the concerns raised regarding the dataset's scope and real-world applicability. My assessment remains flexible and could be adjusted favorably pending a robust and convincing rebuttal.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).

Strength And Weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Peer Review

**Summary of the Submission:**

This paper introduces two primary contributions to the field. Firstly, it presents EAGLE, a substantial 2D fluid simulation dataset featuring dynamic rigid boundaries and Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluid dynamics within 2D environments. Secondly, the authors propose a novel neural network architecture, termed a mesh transformer, designed for solving fluid dynamics problems, and evaluate its performance against established neural network simulators like MeshGraphNet.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

The timely release of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is a significant positive aspect of this work. I anticipate that a high-quality dataset of this nature will effectively stimulate and advance relevant research within the machine learning community.

Conversely, a lingering concern is that the dataset, despite its potential, still feels somewhat underdeveloped. While it likely surpasses many existing fluid simulation datasets detailed in prior publications, I believe there is considerable scope for further refinement and polish.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**

Several aspects of the simulation dataset require further clarification:

*   **RANS Justification:** The rationale behind simulating RANS equations rather than the fundamental Navier-Stokes equations within the dataset is not explicitly stated. Elaboration on this choice would be beneficial.
*   **Coupling Mechanism:** The interaction between the drone and the fluid appears to be unidirectional. Specifically, the drone seems to impose boundary conditions on the fluid's motion, yet the fluid's influence on the drone's movement is not evident (e.g., the absence of velocity or pressure terms in Equation 8). If the fluid indeed has no impact on the drone, the use of Model Predictive Control (MPC) seems somewhat superfluous, and a purely kinematic drone model might suffice.
*   **Boundary Condition Specification:** It is unclear how the drone's mesh precisely defines the boundary conditions for the fluid region. The text suggests that its bounding box might be utilized. A more detailed explanation of the velocity and pressure boundary conditions applied around these bounding boxes is necessary. I had anticipated that specific inlet/outlet flow velocities would be defined at the propeller locations, but such details are missing.
*   **Task Definition Ambiguity:** The task definition presented at the conclusion of Section 3 lacks clarity. Specifically, it is not clear whether the node positions, $x_i$, evolve over time. Furthermore, it appears that the fluid domain undergoes remeshing at each timestep, if my understanding is correct.

Upon my initial reading of the main text, I was under the impression that the proposed dataset involved two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamically controllable UAV, which generated considerable excitement. It would be highly beneficial if the paper could clarify the exact simulation setup early in Section 3 to prevent such misconceptions.

Regarding the proposed network model, the approach of predicting velocity and pressure fields solely based on the current mesh, followed by resampling to a new mesh, appears questionable. It is my understanding that the accurate velocity and pressure fields at the subsequent timestep should inherently be influenced by both the current and the newly formed mesh.

**Reproducibility:** The reproducibility of this work will largely depend on the comprehensive nature of the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation outcomes using Ansys is likely impractical for researchers with limited access to extensive computational resources. Nevertheless, I strongly recommend that the dataset, if possible, includes the precise configurations of the scenes used within Ansys to aid future research.

**Additional Comments:**

*   Could the authors confirm if "EAGLE" is an acronym, and if so, what it stands for?
*   There appears to be some confusion regarding the baselines mentioned. Section 5 initially attributes MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, which seems incorrect. It is more probable that MeshGraphNet refers to the work by Pfaff et al. [2021].
*   I commend the authors for their candor in discussing failure cases and less-than-ideal results, particularly concerning the Scalar flow experiments. This level of transparency is highly valued.

**Summary of the Review:**

While this submission is undoubtedly respectable, my current assessment leads me to lean towards rejection. My score is not yet final, and I remain open to considering alternative perspectives from the authors and fellow reviewers.

**Rating:**

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Consolidator: Mergable Adapter with Group Connections for Visual Adaptation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a consolidator that modifies the pretrained model with the addition of a small set of tunable parameters to store the task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. A two-stage consolidation process is designed by merging corresponding parameters in the training-storage phase and loading-inference phase. Extensive experiments are conducted on various downstream tasks and the results outperform state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

(1) The authors propose a basic module, a consolidator for efficient transfer learning.

(2) A two-stage consolidation process is designed. 

(3) Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks show state-of-the-art performance.

Weaknesses:
This work aims to design a mergeable adapter for efficient transfer learning and proposes a two-stage consolidation process by merging corresponding parameters. I have some questions as follows. 

(1)  In Figure 1, the authors split channels and use zero padding, which might cause a heavy computational cost. Thus, the training time/speed should be shown for comparison with other tuning methods. Besides, I notice that the authors insert a consolidator in the FC layer in Figure 1. Will performance be further enhanced if the consolidator is inserted in other locations? Some recent tuning methods [1, 2] should also be discussed and compared in Table 1. 

(2)  Some other tuning baselines are missing in Table 4. The authors should conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons. 

(3)  The consolidator is designed for a linear layer. Can it be applied to other operational structures, such as self attention. Further, the current consolidator module is designed mainly in the vision transformer. In Table 1, Table 4 and Table 5, the proposed method obtains promising performance compared to the Full tuning. Push it forward, can it be extended to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures [3,4,5]? These will be interesting experiments. Due to the rebuttal time constraints, the authors do not need to add these extension experiments, but feel free to discuss them. 

(4)  There exists a large number of typos in the current version, the authors are able to polish it further. 

[1] ‘AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[2] ‘Scaling & Shifting Your Features: A New Baseline for Efficient Model Tuning’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[3] ‘A ConvNet for the 2020s’, in CVPR2022.

[4] ‘AS-MLP: An Axial Shifted MLP Architecture for Vision’, in ICLR2022.

[5] ‘RepMLPNet: Hierarchical Vision MLP with Re-parameterized Locality’, in CVPR2022.

reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review:

## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Submission:**
This paper introduces a novel consolidator module designed to enhance efficient transfer learning. The proposed consolidator modifies a pre-trained backbone model by incorporating a minimal set of trainable parameters, specifically for storing task-specific knowledge, while the main backbone remains frozen during adaptation. The authors detail a two-stage consolidation procedure, involving the merging of corresponding parameters during the training-storage phase and their subsequent loading for the inference phase. Through extensive experimental validation across a variety of downstream tasks, the method demonstrates superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches, notably achieving this with a reduced number of stored parameters.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**

1.  The core contribution of a dedicated consolidator module for streamlined transfer learning is a significant and promising aspect of this work.
2.  The two-stage consolidation process is well-conceived, offering an organized approach to parameter management and adaptation.
3.  The authors present comprehensive experimental results across diverse downstream tasks, showcasing the method's ability to achieve state-of-the-art performance.

**Weaknesses:**
While the paper presents a compelling approach for efficient transfer learning through its mergeable adapter and two-stage consolidation, several points warrant further consideration and clarification:

1.  Concerning the architectural details presented in Figure 1, the strategy of channel splitting combined with zero padding raises potential concerns regarding computational overhead. It would be highly beneficial to include a comparison of training time or speed against other prevalent tuning methodologies to properly assess this aspect. Furthermore, the current consolidator placement, specifically within the FC layer as depicted in Figure 1, prompts an inquiry into whether alternative insertion points could yield further performance gains. Additionally, Table 1 would be significantly strengthened by the inclusion of a discussion and comparative analysis with recent, highly relevant tuning methods such as AdaptFormer [1] and Scaling & Shifting Your Features [2].
2.  Table 4 appears to omit several pertinent tuning baselines. To ensure a comprehensive and fair comparative evaluation, it is recommended that the authors incorporate additional experiments with these missing tuning methods.
3.  The current design of the consolidator is primarily tailored for linear layers. It would be valuable to explore its applicability to other fundamental operational structures, such as self-attention mechanisms. Moreover, the module's primary application appears to be within Vision Transformers. Given the promising performance against full tuning shown in Tables 1, 4, and 5, investigating the consolidator's extensibility to other model families like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) or various MLP architectures [3,4,5] would constitute fascinating future work. While these extensive experiments are not mandatory for the current rebuttal period due to time constraints, a discussion regarding their potential and feasibility would be greatly appreciated.
4.  The manuscript contains a noticeable number of typographical errors. A thorough proofreading and polishing of the text would significantly enhance its overall presentation and readability.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**
The work demonstrates good clarity in describing the methodology, including specific training details and hyper-parameters, which should facilitate reproducibility. The proposed consolidation process itself represents a novel contribution within the domain of efficient transfer learning. The overall quality of the research is commendable, despite the identified areas for improvement.

**Summary of the Review:**
This paper introduces an innovative two-stage consolidation process for efficient transfer learning that achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer stored parameters. The core idea is sound, and the experimental results are compelling. While there are several areas for improvement, particularly regarding deeper analysis of computational costs, broader architectural comparisons, and text refinement, the merits of the proposed method are substantial. I lean towards accepting this manuscript, contingent on addressing the raised points to further refine its quality.

**Rating:**
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposed **consolidator** to efficiently fine-tune transformers. Consolidator has multi-branches structures, which can be merged into a single matrix and saved on the disk. Extensive experiments demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of consolidator on various vision tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
#### Strength

1. The design of the consolidator is parameter-efficient since its mergeable property. By extending a single branch to multiple branches, the consolidator has better flexibility and transferability.

2.  The two-stage process maximizes the adaptation capacity without extra inference costs.

3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

#### Weaknesses

1. The novelty of this work is limited. The mergeable design is proposed by LoRA [1] for efficient parameter finetuing. Besides, what's the main difference between the **Channel Reorder** and **shuffle** operator in ShuffleNet[2]?

2. Some recent related works are not discussed, e.g. [3][4][5].

3. The improvement is marginal compared with NOAH in Table 1.


[1] Hu, Edward J., et al. "Lora: Low-rank adaptation of large language models." arXiv 2021.

[2] Zhang, Xiangyu, et al. "Shufflenet: An extremely efficient convolutional neural network for mobile devices." CVPR 2018.

[3] Bahng, Hyojin, et al. "Exploring visual prompts for adapting large-scale models." arXiv 2022.

[4] Chen, Shoufa, et al. "AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition." NeurIPS 2022.

[5] Jie, Shibo, and Zhi-Hong Deng. "Convolutional bypasses are better vision transformer adapters." arXiv 2022.

reviews.text:
## Expert Academic Peer Review

**Summary of the Submission:**

This manuscript introduces "Consolidator," a novel method designed for the efficient fine-tuning of transformer models. The core innovation lies in its multi-branch architectural design, which allows these branches to be seamlessly merged into a single matrix for storage, optimizing parameter efficiency. The authors present a comprehensive set of experimental results across various vision tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness and computational advantages of their proposed consolidator.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

#### Strengths

1.  **Parameter Efficiency and Adaptability:** The architecture of the consolidator is inherently parameter-efficient, primarily due to its design which permits the merging of multiple branches. This multi-branch extension also enhances the model's flexibility and transferability across different tasks.
2.  **Optimized Adaptation Capacity:** The proposed two-stage operational process effectively maximizes the model's adaptation capabilities without incurring any additional inference costs, which is a significant practical advantage.
3.  **Clarity and Readability:** The paper is commendably well-structured and written, making it straightforward for readers to follow the proposed methodology and experimental setup.

#### Weaknesses

1.  **Limited Novelty:** A primary concern regarding this work is its perceived limited novelty. The concept of a mergeable design for parameter-efficient fine-tuning has been notably explored and introduced by prior work such as LoRA [1]. Furthermore, the authors need to clearly articulate the fundamental differences between their "Channel Reorder" mechanism and the "shuffle" operator found in ShuffleNet [2].
2.  **Incomplete Related Work Discussion:** The review of related literature appears to be incomplete, as several recent and highly relevant works, specifically [3], [4], and [5], are not discussed or cited. Including these would provide a more comprehensive context for the consolidator's contributions.
3.  **Marginal Performance Gains:** While effective, the reported performance improvements are rather modest. Specifically, when compared against the NOAH baseline in Table 1, the observed gains are quite marginal.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**

The manuscript is presented with commendable clarity, making it easy to comprehend the methods and findings.

A minor typographical error was noted in Section 3.2: "aim **ti**" should be corrected.

Regrettably, without access to the source code, it is currently not possible to fully assess the reproducibility of the reported results.

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper presents an interesting approach for efficient transformer fine-tuning with a well-articulated design and encouraging experimental results. The strengths lie in its parameter efficiency, flexible architecture, and the clear presentation of the work. However, the identified weaknesses regarding the novelty of the core mergeable design, the omission of key related works, and the relatively minor performance improvements against strong baselines prevent it from reaching the acceptance threshold in its current form. Addressing the points on novelty and expanding the related work discussion would significantly strengthen the submission.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The fine-tuning method achieves both efficiency and effectiveness through its design of mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting. Extensive experiments show the method surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning on several transfer learning tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Consolidator is a very effective method. Compared with other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning methods, Consolidator can achieve an almost optimal transfer learning effect.
2. Compared to the adapter methods, the design in this paper can be merged with the original model so that the inference phase does not increase the cost of memory and computation time.

Weakness:
1. The prior of ChannelReorder operation is strong but the explanation of its effectiveness is weak. 
2. The parameters in all tables are stored parameters for Consolidator but not trainable parameters.
3. It should be possible to save time compared to the full parameter finetune during training, I wonder why this is not reported, nor is it compared to other baseline training time.
4. The value of saving storage space is not so great, especially for the base-level model, and there are few experiments on the Large model.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Paper:**
This paper introduces "Consolidator," a novel fine-tuning methodology specifically designed for vision transformers. The proposed approach is highly parameter-efficient, requiring minimal trainable and stored parameters. Its core innovation lies in the integration of mergeable multi-branch architectures and a parameter shifting mechanism, which collectively contribute to both its efficiency and performance. The authors provide comprehensive experimental evidence demonstrating that Consolidator achieves superior results compared to both existing adapter-based techniques and traditional full-parameter fine-tuning across a range of transfer learning tasks.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Exceptional Effectiveness:** The Consolidator method demonstrates impressive effectiveness, consistently achieving near-optimal transfer learning outcomes. Its performance often surpasses that of alternative adapter-based strategies and even full-parameter fine-tuning methods, highlighting its strong capabilities.
2.  **Inference Efficiency:** A notable advantage of this design, particularly when compared to other adapter methods, is its ability to be seamlessly merged back into the original base model. This integration ensures that the inference phase incurs no additional memory overhead or computational latency, offering a significant practical benefit for deployment.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Limited Explanation of ChannelReorder:** While the ChannelReorder operation appears to be founded on a strong conceptual prior, the paper's explanation regarding its precise mechanism and contribution to the overall effectiveness is rather underdeveloped. A more detailed elucidation would greatly enhance the reader's understanding.
2.  **Parameter Reporting Ambiguity:** The parameter counts presented in the tables for Consolidator refer exclusively to *stored* parameters, rather than the more commonly reported *trainable* parameters. This distinction creates potential confusion and should be clarified, with both metrics ideally provided for a complete assessment.
3.  **Missing Training Time Analysis:** Given the parameter-efficient nature of Consolidator, it is reasonable to anticipate substantial reductions in training time compared to full-parameter fine-tuning. However, the manuscript does not report any data on training duration, neither in comparison to full fine-tuning nor against other baseline methods. This omission leaves a significant gap in the experimental evaluation.
4.  **Limited Value in Storage Savings and Large Model Evaluation:** The practical utility of the reported storage space savings seems somewhat limited, especially when considering base-level models. Furthermore, the experimental evaluation lacks sufficient exploration of the method's performance and benefits on larger-scale models, where such efficiencies might be more critically important or where different behavioral characteristics might emerge.
5.  **Proofreading Deficiencies:** The paper contains a noticeable number of minor linguistic issues, including several spelling and grammatical errors. Specific instances include "workss" (P3), "ti" and "thw" (P4), "the all the" (P5), and "accomodate" (P7). A thorough proofread is strongly recommended to improve the manuscript's overall presentation quality.

**Summary of the Review:**
The paper presents an intriguing and highly effective fine-tuning method for Vision Transformers that offers a compelling advantage in terms of inference efficiency due to its innovative mergeable design. While the core idea and empirical results are strong, the manuscript's clarity is hindered by an insufficient explanation of key components (e.g., ChannelReorder). Furthermore, the experimental reporting could be significantly improved by distinguishing between stored and trainable parameters, including an analysis of training time, and expanding the evaluation to larger models. These reporting and clarity issues, coupled with minor editorial errors, indicate that while the work has significant merit, it is not yet fully polished for acceptance.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the adaptation of the well-trained transformer to downstream tasks on resource-limited devices. The authors introduced grouped connections with re-parameterization technology into the training process. The experimental results on serval vision tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
+ Compared to the baselines and other methods, the proposed methods could bring some improvements in accuracy and computation cost.
+ Some ablation experiments are introduced to prove the effectiveness of the proposed method.

The main concerns are listed below. 
- Strictly restricted application scenario. The author introduces the application scenario in the abstract where the resource-limited devices cannot store a full copy of parameters but could provide computation to train/fine-tune the large models. It is a bit strange the devices with the ability to train DL models do not have sufficient storage.
- The proposed method is irrelevant to Transformer. It is closer to the re-parameterization technology. 
- Some recent literature on the adaptation of Transformers is missing.

[a] AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition, NeurIPS 2022.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: I'm Ok with the quality, clarity, and originality of the work.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: As written in the Strength And Weaknesses, the strictly restricted application scenario lowers the practical value of the proposed method.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the adaptation of the well-trained transformer to downstream tasks on resource-limited devices. The authors introduced grouped connections with re-parameterization technology into the training process. The experimental results on serval vision tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Strength And Weaknesses:
+ Compared to the baselines and other methods, the proposed methods could bring some improvements in accuracy and computation cost.
+ Some ablation experiments are introduced to prove the effectiveness of the proposed method.

The main concerns are listed below. 
- Strictly restricted application scenario. The author introduces the application scenario in the abstract where the resource-limited devices cannot store a full copy of parameters but could provide computation to train/fine-tune the large models. It is a bit strange the devices with the ability to train DL models do not have sufficient storage.
- The proposed method is irrelevant to Transformer. It is closer to the re-parameterization technology. 
- Some recent literature on the adaptation of Transformers is missing.

[a] AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition, NeurIPS 2022.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I'm Ok with the quality, clarity, and originality of the work.

Summary Of The Review:
As written in the Strength And Weaknesses, the strictly restricted application scenario lowers the practical value of the proposed method.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Submission:**
This paper investigates strategies for adapting pre-trained Transformer models to various downstream tasks, specifically focusing on environments with limited computational resources. The authors propose an approach that integrates grouped connections with a re-parameterization technique during the model training process. The effectiveness of their method is demonstrated through experimental results conducted on several vision-related tasks.

**Strengths:**
*   The presented method yields noticeable improvements in both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency when compared against established baselines and alternative techniques.
*   The inclusion of ablation experiments effectively supports the claims regarding the contribution and performance of the proposed components.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**
*   A significant concern pertains to the highly specific and somewhat unusual application scenario described. The authors posit a context where resource-constrained devices possess the computational power necessary to train or fine-tune large deep learning models, yet simultaneously lack sufficient storage to house a full set of model parameters. This particular combination of capabilities (strong compute, weak storage) appears to be an uncommon and potentially niche premise.
*   The core methodology introduced seems to be more inherently connected to re-parameterization techniques in general, rather than offering novel insights or direct advancements specifically tailored to Transformer architectures. Its relevance to the Transformer paradigm feels somewhat indirect.
*   The literature review is missing some pertinent and recent works on the adaptation of Transformers. For instance, the discussion should incorporate and reference papers such as "AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition" (NeurIPS 2022).

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**
I find the overall quality, clarity of presentation, and degree of originality in this work to be acceptable.

**Overall Assessment:**
In summary, despite some technical merits, the practical value of the proposed method is significantly constrained by its highly specialized and somewhat peculiar application scenario, as elaborated in the weaknesses section.

**Recommendation:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

This paper studies the adaptation of the well-trained transformer to downstream tasks on resource-limited devices. The authors introduced grouped connections with re-parameterization technology into the training process. The experimental results on serval vision tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Strength And Weaknesses:
+ Compared to the baselines and other methods, the proposed methods could bring some improvements in accuracy and computation cost.
+ Some ablation experiments are introduced to prove the effectiveness of the proposed method.

The main concerns are listed below. 
- Strictly restricted application scenario. The author introduces the application scenario in the abstract where the resource-limited devices cannot store a full copy of parameters but could provide computation to train/fine-tune the large models. It is a bit strange the devices with the ability to train DL models do not have sufficient storage.
- The proposed method is irrelevant to Transformer. It is closer to the re-parameterization technology. 
- Some recent literature on the adaptation of Transformers is missing.

title: Equal Improvability: A New Fairness Notion Considering the Long-term Impact

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments. 

--- post rebuttal --- 
I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel concept of long-term fairness termed "equal improvability" (EI). This metric aims to ensure that individuals from different sensitive groups, when rejected, face comparable "effort required to improve" their standing. The authors theoretically position EI against existing long-term fairness notions like Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse, delineating the conditions under which these different fairness concepts relate. To address EI unfairness, the work proposes three regularization-based optimization methods, each incorporating a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically evaluated using both synthetic and real-world datasets. The experimental section includes an initial set of tests comparing the three mitigation strategies against a fairness-agnostic algorithm on two non-dynamic real-world datasets, followed by a second set of experiments exploring a rudimentary dynamic setting.

### Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths:**
*   The introductory sections, including the initial related work, are particularly well-crafted. They effectively underscore the significance of the proposed EI metric and clearly differentiate it from other established fairness measures in the field.
*   The paper benefits from clear explanations and supplementary materials, such as the detailed elaborations in the appendix and illustrative figures, which significantly aid reader comprehension. The specific example provided for $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ following Definition 2.1 was notably helpful.
*   The authors responsibly present their empirical findings for the fairness-based regularization algorithms, acknowledging that these methods primarily aim to mitigate EI unfairness without offering stronger theoretical guarantees on the solutions obtained. This measured approach is commendable.
*   Section 3 of the paper is structured logically and written with excellent clarity, making it easy to follow.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A notable omission in the first set of experiments is the lack of comparison between the proposed long-term fairness metrics and other existing long-term fairness baselines, beyond the basic fairness-unaware ERM. Given that these are arguably single-step episodes (a subset of n-step scenarios), and that methods exist for other long-term fairness metrics (e.g., for Equal Recourse), incorporating these would provide a more robust comparative analysis.
*   The paper does not adequately address the practical challenges of mitigating unfairness in scenarios involving multiple sensitive groups, which are common in real-world applications (e.g., considering race and gender simultaneously). Merely adding multiple pairwise fairness penalties to an objective function might incentivize fairness but could also allow violations if doing so significantly benefits the primary objective. Qualitatively, determining and scaling the importance of each additional pairwise penalty relative to the main objective appears to be a complex and non-trivial task for practitioners.
*   Extending the previous point, the covariance-based EI penalty, inspired by Zafar's work, relies on proxies for the original fairness constraint. However, the theoretical guarantee that solutions found by optimizing this proxy objective indeed satisfy the original constraint is not provided. This lack of guarantees for solutions derived from regularization-based approaches (which might also apply to the KDE-based penalty, though I am less familiar with its origins) represents a fundamental limitation of this methodology. I suggest that the authors explicitly discuss this inherent weakness of using fairness-based regularization in the main body of the paper.

### Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

**Clarity:** The paper is generally easy to read and understand. The proofs I briefly reviewed in Appendix A appear clear. However, clarification is needed regarding the experimental results on page 6: the "statistics for five trials" are mentioned, but it's unclear where these statistics are presented, and whether the error bars represent standard error or standard deviation.

**Reproducibility:** The necessary information for reproducing the experiments, including hyperparameters, is stated to be available in the appendix. While I did not inspect the code, I am confident that the experiments could be reconstructed based on the provided details.

**Quality/Novelty:** The authors effectively demonstrate that their proposed notion of fairness is related to but distinct from existing long-term metrics. The three algorithms introduced, while being incremental modifications of prior work utilizing soft constraints (i.e., regularization penalties), serve as valuable stepping stones for future research. It is important to reiterate that these methods, as noted, do not offer strong guarantees on the fairness properties of the solutions they yield.

### Minor Issues and Suggested Improvements

*   **Appendix B, first sentence:** Please correct the grammatical error: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" should be "we explain what each term in the EI definition means".
*   **Related Work Sections:** I only realized the existence of a second related work section towards the end of the paper. It would improve navigation if this were signposted or mentioned in the initial related work section.
*   **"First-order" Definition:** The term "first-order" is used but never explicitly defined. Readers are left to infer its meaning. Does it imply that these metrics consider potential follow-up inequity risk one "time-step" or one decision into the future? The experiments present both a one-step scenario (first set) and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Clarification is needed on whether the mentioned long-term metrics are indeed n-step fairness notions.
*   **EI Disparity Definition:** To enhance clarity and reduce confusion, I suggest defining "EI disparity" directly in the main body of the paper. Currently, the authors refer to Appendix B.3 for its definition but then use the term frequently throughout the main text.

### Summary of the Review

I recommend **acceptance** for this paper. The proposed approach is well-motivated, and the authors meticulously highlight how their metric differentiates itself within the landscape of long-term fairness. A strength of the work is the authors' candidness regarding the limitations of their three optimization algorithms, specifically that they offer no guarantees that the derived solutions will definitively mitigate EI unfairness. While this is a known characteristic of soft constraints, it remains crucial to discuss this limitation more explicitly in the main text, as detailed in the weaknesses section. Additionally, a few clarifying points regarding the experimental setup, particularly the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments and the presentation of statistical measures, would further strengthen the work.

--- post rebuttal ---
I appreciate the thorough response provided by the authors. They have committed to addressing many of my concerns in their revised manuscript, which I believe will significantly enhance the clarity and impact of the work. Following discussions with other reviewers, my stance remains in favor of acceptance.

### Rating:
**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the delayed impact of fairness. The authors introduce a notion of fairness called \emph{equal improvability}, which, subject to a bounded amount of improvement, equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations. The authors aim to solve the typical loss minimization problem of classification subject to the equal improbability constraint. Since the constraint is non-convex, the paper proposes 3 different approaches to solve the constrained optimization. The paper concludes with empirical analysis both in the one-shot game as well as the repeated setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
Strengths:
------------------------------------------
-- The delayed impact of fairness in machine learning has not received much attention in the community (mainly due to the hardness of modeling this impact) so in that sense, it is great to see papers on this topic.

--The paper is pretty well written and it is clear to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The delayed impact notion of fairness is natural and sensible.

------------------------------------------
Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
-- The main technical contribution of the paper is the definition. Hence, the technical novelty (when enforcing the equal improvability constraint) is limited. 

-- Section 4.2 contains the most interesting part of the paper, though some of the details are hard to follow/justify. It would be great to justify the choice of $\epsilon(x)$ as well as describe in more detail how the updates to the classifier are done.

------------------------------------------
Minor Comments:
------------------------------------------
-- Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 show that equal improvability and bounded effort are mathematically different. It would be nice to 
describe this in more detail as it is not clear to me how to set the reward function in Heidari et al., 2019 to connect the two works.

-- Are there deeper connections between equal recourse and equal improvability that are overlooked? If not, is not it surprising that these two methods work exactly the same in the experiments in section 4.2?

-- Please stop using the Adult dataset: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html

-- The Y value in Figure 4 should be bounded by 1. Why is it above one in the left two sub-figures?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Submission:**

This paper delves into the long-term ramifications of fairness considerations in machine learning systems. The authors introduce a novel fairness criterion termed "equal improvability." This concept aims to ensure that, given a predefined capacity for improvement, the likelihood of acceptance for individuals who were initially rejected is uniformly distributed across different population groups. The core objective is to tackle the standard classification loss minimization problem, but with the added "equal improvability" constraint. Recognizing the non-convex nature of this constraint, the work proposes three distinct methodologies for solving the resulting constrained optimization task. The paper concludes with an empirical evaluation of these approaches in both single-shot and sequential decision-making scenarios.

**Strengths:**

*   **Timely and Important Topic:** The investigation into the delayed or long-term effects of fairness in machine learning is a critical, yet often under-explored, area within the research community. This is primarily due to the inherent complexity in accurately modeling such impacts. Therefore, this paper's contribution to this nascent field is highly commendable.
*   **Clarity and Intuition:** The paper is generally well-structured and lucidly written, making its overarching concepts and methodologies straightforward to grasp. The proposed notion of delayed fairness, "equal improvability," is presented in a manner that feels both natural and intuitively sound.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Limited Technical Novelty in Constraint Enforcement:** While the definition of "equal improvability" itself constitutes a novel conceptual contribution, the technical approaches employed for enforcing this specific constraint within the optimization framework appear somewhat limited in their innovative aspects.
*   **Details in Section 4.2 Require Elaboration:** Section 4.2 presents what is arguably the most compelling segment of the paper. However, certain implementation details within this section are challenging to fully comprehend or justify. Specifically, a more thorough explanation regarding the rationale behind selecting $\epsilon(x)$ would be beneficial, as would a more detailed description of the mechanism by which the classifier updates are performed.

**Minor Comments:**

*   **Distinguishing Fairness Notions:** Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 highlight a mathematical divergence between "equal improvability" and "bounded effort." It would be valuable if the authors could expand on this distinction. Furthermore, it remains unclear how one might configure the reward function in the work by Heidari et al. (2019) to draw a clearer connection between these two concepts.
*   **Connections to Equal Recourse:** The paper should explore whether there are deeper, perhaps overlooked, connections between "equal recourse" and "equal improvability." If such connections are absent, the observed identical performance of these two methods in the experiments presented in Section 4.2 is rather surprising and warrants further discussion.
*   **Dataset Choice:** Please consider refraining from using the Adult dataset in future research, as detailed in the NeurIPS 2021 paper linked here: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html.
*   **Figure 4 Y-axis:** In Figure 4, the Y-axis values for the two leftmost sub-figures inexplicably exceed one. As these values typically represent probabilities or proportions, they should logically be bounded by one. This inconsistency requires correction or clarification.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**

The paper demonstrates good clarity, allowing readers to readily follow its main conceptual threads. Its relevance to the machine learning fairness community is clear. The definitions introduced are novel, though, as noted, a more explicit and detailed discussion of their connections to existing literature would enhance the work. The authors have provided code, which aids in reproducibility.

**Overall Assessment:**

I found this paper to be an engaging read. It represents a valuable contribution to the growing body of literature concerning the delayed implications of fairness in algorithmic decision-making. While the technical novelty, particularly in the methods for enforcing the proposed constraint, is not exceptionally strong, I believe the paper's exploration of an important and challenging topic makes it a suitable addition to the ICLR program.

**Post-rebuttal Update:**

I sincerely appreciate the authors' comprehensive responses to my queries. Having also reviewed the other expert assessments and the authors' rebuttals, I acknowledge that there's a consensus that this work could be on the cusp of acceptance or rejection. However, my position remains (slightly) leaning towards acceptance.

**Rating:**

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the delayed impact of fairness. The authors introduce a notion of fairness called \emph{equal improvability}, which, subject to a bounded amount of improvement, equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations. The authors aim to solve the typical loss minimization problem of classification subject to the equal improbability constraint. Since the constraint is non-convex, the paper proposes 3 different approaches to solve the constrained optimization. The paper concludes with empirical analysis both in the one-shot game as well as the repeated setting.

Strength And Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
Strengths:
------------------------------------------
-- The delayed impact of fairness in machine learning has not received much attention in the community (mainly due to the hardness of modeling this impact) so in that sense, it is great to see papers on this topic.

--The paper is pretty well written and it is clear to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The delayed impact notion of fairness is natural and sensible.

------------------------------------------
Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
-- The main technical contribution of the paper is the definition. Hence, the technical novelty (when enforcing the equal improvability constraint) is limited. 

-- Section 4.2 contains the most interesting part of the paper, though some of the details are hard to follow/justify. It would be great to justify the choice of $\epsilon(x)$ as well as describe in more detail how the updates to the classifier are done.

------------------------------------------
Minor Comments:
------------------------------------------
-- Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 show that equal improvability and bounded effort are mathematically different. It would be nice to 
describe this in more detail as it is not clear to me how to set the reward function in Heidari et al., 2019 to connect the two works.

-- Are there deeper connections between equal recourse and equal improvability that are overlooked? If not, is not it surprising that these two methods work exactly the same in the experiments in section 4.2?

-- Please stop using the Adult dataset: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html

-- The Y value in Figure 4 should be bounded by 1. Why is it above one in the left two sub-figures?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.
